title: 10 best herbal tinctures for bad breath

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Peppermint tinctures helps with bad breath because of its natural antibacterial properties, which help to eliminate bacteria that cause bad breath. The menthol in peppermint also helps to freshen the mouth and throat by stimulating saliva production, which washes away bacteria and food particles that can contribute to bad breath. Additionally, peppermint's cooling effect on the tongue and roof of the mouth can also help to reduce the sensation of dryness, further reducing the likelihood of bad breath.
remedy_properties: Digestive aid: Peppermint oil's carminative and anti-inflammatory properties help soothe digestive issues, reducing the likelihood of halitosis caused by poor digestion or gum inflammation.

Antibacterial properties: Peppermint's menthol content has antibacterial effects that can help combat oral bacteria responsible for bad breath, reducing the formation of volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) that cause bad breath.
Mouthwash-like effects: The tincture's antimicrobial and astringent properties can help kill bacteria on contact, leaving a fresh, clean sensation in the mouth that helps eliminate bad breath.
remedy_parts: Roots: Peppermint roots have antibacterial and antifungal properties, making them effective in reducing bacteria that causes bad breath.
Seeds: Peppermint seeds contain essential oils that help to freshen breath and reduce inflammation in the mouth, helping to alleviate bad breath.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality peppermint leaves mentha x piperita and dry them properly within three days.
Combine one part dried peppermint leaves with two parts vodka in a clean glass jar with tight-fitting lid.
Steep the mixture in a cool dark place for six to eight weeks, shaking the jar daily.
Strain the liquid through a cheesecloth into another clean glass bottle, discarding the solids after one hour.
Store the peppermint tincture in an amber glass bottle with tight-fitting lid at room temperature for up to two years.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Tea tree tinctures helps with bad breath because of its antimicrobial properties, which target and eliminate the bacteria that cause halitosis. The tannins in tea tree essential oil also help to reduce inflammation in the mouth and gums, promoting a healthy oral environment. Additionally, tea tree tincture's antiviral and antibacterial properties help to combat viral infections, such as thrush, which can also contribute to bad breath. By addressing the underlying causes of bad breath, tea tree tinctures provide a natural and effective solution for freshening your breath.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial activity: Tea tree oil has been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria, viruses, and fungi that contribute to bad breath (halitosis), helping to reduce their presence in the mouth.
Anti-inflammatory effects: The terpenes present in tea tree oil have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce swelling and inflammation in the gums and tongue, common causes of bad breath.

Antioxidant properties: Tea tree oil's antioxidant properties can help neutralize free radicals that can damage oral tissues and contribute to bad breath by creating a favorable environment for bacterial growth.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the tea tree plant contain compounds like cineole, which has antibacterial properties that help combat bad breath-causing bacteria.
Leaves: Tea tree leaves have been traditionally used to treat oral health issues, including bad breath, due to their antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh leaves of melaleuca alternifolia and wash them thoroughly under cool running water.
Chop the washed leaves into small pieces and combine them with 70% vodka in a clean glass jar.
Allow the mixture to steep for 14 days, shaking the jar daily to facilitate extraction.
Strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or coffee filter into another clean glass jar, discarding the solids.
Store the tincture in a cool dark place and use it within 2 years, dosing 20-30 drops as needed for bad breath relief.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Tasmanian blue gum tinctures helps with bad breath because of its natural antibacterial properties, which effectively combat the underlying causes of halitosis. The tincture's active compounds, such as eucalyptol and cineole, have been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria that contribute to bad breath, while also freshening the mouth and reducing gum inflammation. As a result, regular use of Tasmanian blue gum tinctures can help eliminate unpleasant odors and leave you with a fresher, cleaner feeling in your mouth.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: Tasmanian Blue Gum's antimicrobial compounds, such as eucalyptol and α-pinene, help combat bacteria in the mouth that can cause bad breath by reducing their growth and eliminating them.
Anti-inflammatory properties: The tincture's anti-inflammatory compounds may help reduce inflammation in the gums and mouth tissues, which is a common underlying cause of bad breath.

Astringent and antiseptic properties: Tasmanian Blue Gum's astringent and antiseptic compounds can help dry up excess mucus and saliva, reducing the amount of volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) that can contribute to bad breath.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and antimicrobial compounds that help combat bacterial growth and freshen breath.
Barks: Contains flavonoids and terpenoids with antibacterial properties, making it effective against oral bacteria causing bad breath.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh tasmanian blue gum leaves in the early morning for optimal potency and aroma retention.
Cleanly chop 250 grams of harvested leaves into small pieces to facilitate extraction.
Combine chopped leaves with 500 milliliters of high-proof vodka in a clean glass jar, ensuring leaves are completely submerged.
Store the jar in a cool dark place for 6-8 weeks, shaking it daily, to allow for slow and controlled extraction of active compounds.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or coffee filter into another container, discarding solids and transferring liquid tincture to glass bottles with tight-fitting lids for storage.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Ceylon cinnamon tinctures helps with bad breath because of its potent antimicrobial properties, which target the underlying causes of halitosis. The tincture's active compounds, including cinnamaldehyde and cinnamic acid, inhibit the growth of bacteria that contribute to bad breath, reducing volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) production in the mouth. Additionally, Ceylon cinnamon's anti-inflammatory properties soothe gum inflammation and reduce plaque formation, further combating bad breath. By addressing the root causes of halitosis, herbal Ceylon cinnamon tinctures offer a natural solution for fresher breath.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial activity: Ceylon cinnamon tincture's antimicrobial properties help to kill bacteria in the mouth and throat that can cause bad breath, reducing their population and eliminating their ability to produce volatile sulfur compounds that contribute to halitosis.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory effects of Ceylon cinnamon tincture may help to reduce swelling and irritation in the gums and tongue, which can contribute to bad breath by creating a favorable environment for bacterial growth.
Antioxidant activity: Antioxidants in Ceylon cinnamon tincture help to neutralize free radicals that can damage oral tissues and contribute to bad breath, promoting overall oral health and freshness.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: Rich in essential oils, particularly cinnamaldehyde, which has antibacterial properties that help combat bacteria causing bad breath.
Barks: Contains a compound called cinnamic acid, which has natural antiseptic and anti-inflammatory effects, helping to reduce inflammation in the mouth and gums.
Seeds: Rich in antioxidants and flavonoids, which can help neutralize free radicals and reduce oxidative stress that may contribute to bad breath.
remedy_recipe: Gather high-quality cinnamomum verum bark, vodka or grain alcohol, and glass jars with lids for the tincture process.
Chop the dried ceylon cinnamon bark into small pieces and soak them in a clean glass jar with 20% vodka or grain alcohol for at least 2 weeks.
Shake the jar twice daily to ensure the tincture mixture is well-combined and left it for 4-6 weeks, allowing the active compounds to extract into the liquid.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or coffee filter into another clean glass jar, discarding the solids and retaining the cinnamon-infused liquid.
Store the final tincture in dark glass jars with tight-fitting lids in a cool place for up to 2 years, using 10-20 drops sublingually or added to water as needed.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Ginger tinctures helps with bad breath because it has natural antibacterial properties that help to kill bacteria in the mouth, which is a common cause of bad breath. The essential oils present in ginger also have anti-inflammatory properties, reducing swelling and redness in the gums and tongue, which can contribute to bad breath. Additionally, ginger tinctures stimulate saliva production, washing away bacteria and debris from the teeth and tongue, leaving the mouth feeling fresh and clean.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: Ginger's antimicrobial compounds, such as gingerols and shogaols, help to combat the growth of bacteria in the mouth, which can contribute to bad breath.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Ginger's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as gingerols and shogaols, reduce inflammation in the gums and mucous membranes, which can help alleviate symptoms of gum disease and halitosis (bad breath).
Digestive aid: Ginger's ability to stimulate digestion and relieve nausea can help to prevent the buildup of toxins and debris in the mouth, which can cause bad breath.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: These are the underground stems that contain high concentrations of bioactive compounds like gingerols and shogaols, which have natural antibacterial properties that help combat bad breath.
Barks: The barks of the ginger plant contain a type of lignan called sesquiterpene, which has been shown to possess antimicrobial activity against oral pathogens responsible for bad breath.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh ginger roots and wash them thoroughly with cold water to remove dirt and debris.
Peel the ginger roots and slice them thinly into small pieces weighing approximately 2-3 ounces per jar.
Combine the sliced ginger with a mixture of 70% organic vodka and 30% filtered water in a clean glass jar filling it almost to the top.
Store the jar in a cool dark place for four weeks allowing the mixture to steep and infuse.
Strain the tincture through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass bottle discarding the solids and labeling the bottle with the date and ingredients.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Coriander tinctures helps with bad breath because of its natural antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties. The tincture contains compounds like cineole and linolool that have been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria in the mouth, reducing volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) that cause bad breath. Additionally, coriander's anti-inflammatory effects help to reduce gum inflammation and swelling, which can also contribute to bad breath. As a result, regular use of herbal coriander tinctures may leave your breath feeling fresh and clean.
remedy_properties: Antibacterial properties: Coriander's antibacterial compounds, such as linalool and geraniol, help combat the bacteria that cause bad breath by inhibiting their growth and reducing their ability to produce volatile sulfur compounds.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Coriander's anti-inflammatory compounds, including flavonoids and phenolic acids, reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, which can contribute to bad breath and gum disease.

Antioxidant properties: Coriander's antioxidants, such as vitamin C and beta-carotene, neutralize free radicals that can damage oral tissues and contribute to bad breath, while also boosting overall immunity.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are rich in antimicrobial properties, which help eliminate bacteria and fungi responsible for bad breath.
Seeds: Coriander seeds have natural antibacterial compounds that inhibit the growth of odor-causing microorganisms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh coriander leaves and stems from mature plants, ensuring cleanliness throughout.
Chop the harvested material into small pieces, weighing out 100 grams for this batch.
Combine chopped coriander with 500 milliliters of high-proof vodka in a glass jar, sealing tightly.
Store the mixture in a cool, dark place for six weeks, shaking occasionally during that time.
Strain the liquid through cheesecloth or a coffee filter, discarding solids and transferring to small glass bottles.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Thyme tinctures helps with bad breath because of its potent antimicrobial properties, which work to eliminate bacteria and fungi that contribute to halitosis. The bioactive compounds in thyme, such as thymol and carvacrol, have been shown to inhibit the growth of oral pathogens, reducing volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) responsible for unpleasant odors. Regular use of a herbal thyme tincture can help freshen breath, soothe gum inflammation, and leave the mouth feeling clean and refreshed.
remedy_properties: Antibacterial properties: Thyme's essential oils, such as thymol, have been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria in the mouth and throat, reducing the production of volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) responsible for bad breath.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Thyme tincture may help reduce inflammation in the gums and mucous membranes, which can contribute to bad breath by minimizing irritation and discomfort that can lead to increased VSC production.

Antioxidant properties: Thyme's antioxidants, such as flavonoids and phenolic acids, can help protect against oxidative stress and damage in the oral cavity, reducing the likelihood of bad breath caused by free radicals.
remedy_parts: Roots: Thyme roots contain a high concentration of antimicrobial compounds that help to eliminate bacteria causing bad breath.
Seeds: Thyme seeds are rich in antioxidants and have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to reduce inflammation in the mouth and gums that contributes to bad breath.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality thymus vulgaris leaves and stems, ensuring they are free from contaminants.
Dry the thyme material in a well-ventilated area or using a food dehydrator until crispy.
Crush the dried thyme into small pieces using a mortar and pestle to release its essential oils.
Combine one part crushed thyme with 5 parts high-proof vodka in a clean glass jar, letting it macerate for six weeks, shaking daily.
Strain the tincture through a coffee filter or cheesecloth into another container, discarding solids, and store in dark glass bottles at room temperature.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Wild marjoram tinctures helps with bad breath because of its natural antibacterial properties, which combat the growth of bacteria that can cause halitosis. The herb's essential oils, such as camphor and bornyl acetate, have been shown to inhibit the production of volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) responsible for unpleasant odors. Additionally, wild marjoram tinctures may help reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, which can also contribute to bad breath. By using herbal wild marjoram tinctures, individuals may experience a fresher, cleaner sensation in their mouths, leading to improved oral hygiene and confidence.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: Wild marjoram's antimicrobial properties help to kill bacteria in the mouth and throat, reducing the production of volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) that cause bad breath.
Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory properties of wild marjoram tincture can help reduce inflammation in the gums and mucous membranes, which can contribute to bad breath.
Antioxidant properties: Wild marjoram's antioxidant properties help to neutralize free radicals that can damage oral tissues and lead to bad breath.
remedy_parts: Roots: Known for their anti-inflammatory properties, the roots help reduce gum inflammation and ease discomfort associated with bad breath.
Leaves: Rich in antioxidants and flavonoids, the leaves provide a natural antibacterial effect that targets bacteria causing bad breath.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh wild marjoram leaves at dawn or mid-morning when the essential oils are most concentrated.
Wash the harvested leaves thoroughly in cold water and gently pat dry with a clean towel.
Combine one part dried wild marjoram leaves with 5 parts high-proof vodka in a clean glass jar, filling to the top.
Seal the jar and store it in a dark place for four to six weeks, shaking the mixture daily to facilitate extraction.
Strain the tincture through a coffee filter or cheesecloth into a clean glass bottle, discarding the solids and labeling the tincture as "wild marjoram tincture".

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Summer savory tinctures helps with bad breath because of its natural antibacterial and antifungal properties. The herb's essential oils, such as carvacrol and thymol, have been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria that contribute to halitosis. When taken as a tincture, summer savory can help reduce the population of these odor-causing microbes in the mouth, leaving breath fresher and cleaner. Additionally, its anti-inflammatory properties may soothe gum inflammation and reduce plaque buildup, further contributing to improved oral health and fresher breath.
remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: Summer savory tincture's antimicrobial properties help to kill bacteria and other microorganisms that can cause bad breath, reducing odor-causing compounds in the mouth.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Summer savory's anti-inflammatory effects may help reduce inflammation in the gums and mucous membranes of the mouth, which can contribute to bad breath, as well as alleviate discomfort and pain associated with gum disease or other oral infections.

Digestive aid: Summer savory tincture has been traditionally used to stimulate digestive enzymes and relieve indigestion and bloating, which are common causes of bad breath, by promoting proper digestion and reducing the growth of harmful bacteria in the gut.
remedy_parts: Roots: Summer savory roots have antimicrobial properties, making them effective in reducing bacteria that cause bad breath.
Buds: The buds of the summer savory plant contain oils that help to reduce inflammation and kill bacteria in the mouth, tongue, and throat.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh satureja hortensis leaves and flowers in the morning when they are at peak potency.
Dry the harvested material in a warm dark place for 3-7 days or until it reaches a brittle consistency.
Crush 200g of dried satureja hortensis into smaller pieces using a mortar and pestle to release its oils and essences.
Steep 100ml of vodka in a clean glass jar with the crushed herbs for 2 weeks, shaking daily to facilitate extraction.
Strain the tincture through a coffee filter or cheesecloth into a dark glass bottle, discarding the solids and storing in a cool place.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Rosemary tinctures helps with bad breath because of its natural antibacterial properties, which target the underlying causes of halitosis. The essential oils in rosemary tincture, such as camphor and borneol, work to eliminate the bacteria that thrive on the surface of the tongue and gums, reducing their ability to produce sulfur compounds that cause bad breath. Additionally, rosemary's natural astringent properties help to dry up excess saliva and reduce bacterial growth, leaving the mouth feeling fresh and clean.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: Rosemary's antioxidants help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the mouth, which can contribute to bad breath by breaking down food particles and bacteria.

Antibacterial properties: Rosemary's essential oils have antibacterial effects on various oral bacteria, including those that cause bad breath (halitosis), thereby reducing their numbers and preventing the formation of volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) that cause bad breath.

Stimulant properties: Rosemary's stimulants can help improve blood flow to the mouth, tongue, and throat, which may enhance saliva production and help wash away bacteria, food particles, and other debris that can contribute to bad breath.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, making them effective in reducing gum inflammation and bad breath.
Seeds: Containing volatile oils with antibacterial properties, helping to combat oral bacteria causing bad breath.
remedy_recipe: Gather fresh rosemary leaves and clean them thoroughly to remove any dirt or debris.
Combine one part rosemary leaves with two parts vodka in a glass jar for a 4-week maceration period.
Steep the mixture in a cool dark place at room temperature shaking the jar daily for optimal extraction.
Strain the tincture through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean glass bottle discarding the solids.
Store the finished tincture in the refrigerator and allow it to mature for 2 weeks before use as a bad breath remedy.

intro_desc: Herbal tinctures for bad breath are concentrated liquid extracts made from various plants, herbs, and botanicals that have been used for centuries to freshen breath and alleviate halitosis. These natural remedies help by reducing bacteria in the mouth, neutralizing volatile sulfur compounds, and soothing gums and tongue. Examples of effective herbal tinctures include peppermint, eucalyptus, and sage, which can be taken sublingually or added to water for a refreshing rinse. By using these tinctures, individuals can enjoy improved oral health, increased confidence in social interactions, and a sense of overall well-being.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat bad breath with herbal tinctures is to combine a few drops of peppermint, sage, and tea tree oil tinctures in water or saliva to create an antibacterial and anti-inflammatory mouthwash. Take 5-10 drops three times a day before meals or as needed. This natural remedy helps reduce bacteria that cause bad breath, while also soothing gum inflammation and freshening the breath.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to bad breath that are treated with herbal tinctures are halitosis-like conditions such as gum inflammation, tooth decay, and mouth sores. Herbal remedies like peppermint, clove, and eucalyptus help reduce oral bacteria, fight infections, and soothe gums. Other conditions treated with herbal tinctures include tonsil stones, gum recession, and dry mouth, all of which can contribute to unpleasant breath odor.
